Data-Driven Streaming Content Planning Through Audience Reaction Analysis

Revised Report

Item Information

Team Members 2026390071 이가원 / 2026390118 옥윤수 / 2026390125 편서욱 / 2026390146 안유정

Project Theme Data-driven YouTube/Netflix content planning

GitHub Repository https://yujung6221.github.io/my.website/

Revision Focus

This revised version reorganizes the original report so that the logic becomes clearer: data collection -> text-mining evidence -> findings -> one integrated content proposal. The revision also adds clearer selection criteria, explains the characteristics of each data source, separates data cleaning from analysis preprocessing, provides captions for all figures, and discusses limitations. Peer feedback about API/tools, sarcasm, genre balance, quantitative evidence, and proposal differentiation is also reflected in the revised methodology and proposal sections.

1. Introduction

Streaming platforms such as Netflix, YouTube, and short-form video services have changed how audiences discover, evaluate, and share entertainment content. Viewers no longer respond only through ratings or box-office numbers. They also leave comments, post recommendation videos, write reviews, create memes, and discuss theories online. For content planners, these user- generated reactions are useful because they reveal what viewers emotionally remember and what they are willing to share. The purpose of this project is to identify patterns in online audience reactions and use those patterns to design a future streaming content idea for StreamKUS. Instead of relying only on intuition, our team analyzed online comments and reviews related to popular films and series. We focused especially on emotional engagement, relatability, and online discussion potential because these appeared repeatedly in viral streaming culture. The guiding research questions are: (1) Which emotions and themes appear most often in successful online content discussions? (2) How do reactions differ by genre or content type? (3) How can these patterns be translated into a practical content proposal?

2. Data Collection and Dataset Design

2.1 Why online audience reactions were collected

Online comments were selected because they show spontaneous audience reactions more directly than formal surveys. Reviews on IMDb or Rotten Tomatoes often contain relatively organized evaluations, while YouTube, TikTok, and Reddit comments include short emotional expressions, memes, slang, and immediate reactions. By combining these different sources, the dataset could capture both structured opinions and casual viral behavior.

2.2 Data sources and their roles

Source Main analytical value

Immediate reactions to trailers, clips, reviews, and YouTube comments recommendation videos. Useful for identifying emotional words and shareable scenes. Longer and more structured opinions. Useful for IMDb / Rotten Tomatoes user reviews understanding why viewers liked or disliked the whole work. Thread-based discussion, theories, interpretation, and Reddit discussions fandom debate. Useful for measuring discussion potential. Recommendation trends, short emotional reactions, TikTok / short-form posts and repostable scenes. Useful for identifying viral shareability. Fan opinions, genre-specific discussion, and repeated Online movie communities topic patterns. Useful for checking whether a reaction appears across communities.

2.3 Selection criteria

To make the selection process clearer, the revised report defines the criteria used to choose works. A work was included when it satisfied at least two of the following conditions: it had a large number of accessible public comments or reviews, it appeared repeatedly in recommendation or discussion content, it generated clear emotional or theory-based reactions, and it represented one of the major genres needed for comparison. “High viewer engagement” therefore means not simply popularity, but visible audience activity such as repeated comments, reviews, theory discussions, or short-form sharing. Most works were selected from 2018-2025 to match recent streaming culture. Up (2009) was treated as a reference case rather than a recent trend case because it is a well-known emotional animation that helps compare newer healing or family-oriented content with an older benchmark.

Selected work Year Genre / type Reason for inclusion Everything Everywhere Emotional family conflict, All at Once 2022 Comedy / Sci-Fi / Drama identity, multiverse discussion Inside Out 2 2024 Animation / Family Anxiety, adolescence, emotional relatability Soul 2020 Animation / Drama Life meaning, healing, self-reflection Reference case for Up 2009 Animation / Adventure emotional animation and family loss Wednesday 2022 Mystery / Comedy Fan theories, character identity, online memes Squid Game 2021 Thriller / Drama Social criticism, survival tension, global discussion Parasite 2019 Thriller / Drama Class conflict, plot twist, social interpretation Don't Look Up 2021 Comedy / Satire Political and social commentary Red Notice 2021 Action / Comedy Star power, action scenes, light entertainment Obsession, performance Whiplash 2014 Drama / Music pressure, intense character conflict F1 The Movie 2025 Action / Sports Racing spectacle, sports fandom, star appeal KPop Demon Hunters 2025 Animation / Musical Music, fandom, pop- culture shareability GOAT 2024 Action / Sports Fan recommendations and entertainment value Apex 2025 Action / Thriller Plot-driven discussion and star/genre appeal Anticipated science-fiction Project Hail Mary 2026 Sci-Fi / Adventure and problem-solving narrative Youth drama sample A 2018-2025 Coming-of-Age / Drama Academic pressure and identity struggle Mystery series sample B 2018-2025 Mystery / Thriller Hidden clues and episode- by-episode speculation Healing drama sample C 2018-2025 Drama / Slice of Life Comfort, friendship, and emotional recovery

Figure 1. Genre tags included in the selected works. Because several works belong to more than one genre,

the total number of genre tags is larger than the number of works. This figure is placed in the data section because it explains dataset composition, not a finding by itself.

2.4 Collection tools and validation

The data were collected through a mixed process. Publicly available comments and reviews were gathered from platform pages and, when possible, through official or public search functions. For platforms where API access was limited or unnecessary for this class project, the team used manual sampling and recorded the source, title, platform, and date range. The team did not use private data or restricted user information. The combined dataset contained approximately 4,000 public comments and reviews from 18 works. To reduce selection bias, reactions were collected from more than one platform whenever possible. The same content idea was considered stronger only when similar keywords or reactions appeared across different source types, such as both YouTube comments and review pages.

2.5 Cleaning and preprocessing during data collection

Data cleaning was performed before text mining so that unrelated or duplicated text would not distort keyword frequency. Duplicate comments were removed when the same sentence appeared repeatedly under the same source. Spam was removed when a comment was unrelated to the work, such as promotional links, bot-like advertisements, or comments consisting only of random symbols. Very short expressions with no interpretable meaning were excluded, while short emotional comments such as “I cried” were kept because they directly showed audience reaction. Repeated emotional expressions were standardized to improve consistency. For example, “soooo sad,” “so sad,” and “sad…” were grouped under the basic term “sad.” Similar but not identical words such as “comforting,” “heartwarming,” and “healing” were not erased; instead, they were coded under a broader emotional category when interpreting themes. Sarcasm and memes were handled carefully: clearly sarcastic comments were checked manually when they affected sentiment, and unclear cases were marked as neutral rather than automatically positive or negative.

3. Text Mining Methods and Results

This section reports the actual analytical procedure and evidence. In the original report, some figures appeared without enough explanation. In this revision, each figure is connected to a specific interpretation. The text-mining process consisted of cleaning, tokenization, stop-word removal, keyword frequency analysis, sentiment/theme coding, and cluster visualization.

3.1 Preprocessing procedure

The preprocessing in Section 2 removed irrelevant data during collection. The preprocessing in this section prepared the remaining text for analysis. The distinction is important: Section 2 cleaning decides what data belong in the dataset, while Section 3 preprocessing changes the included text into analyzable words. Example preprocessing logic: 1. Convert text to lowercase. 2. Remove URLs, emojis, repeated punctuation, and meaningless symbols. 3. Tokenize sentences into words. 4. Remove stop words such as “the,” “and,” “really,” and “very.” 5. Normalize repeated letters and repeated emotional words. 6. Save cleaned text with source, title, genre, and date information.

3.2 Keyword frequency and topic clusters

Keyword frequency analysis was used to find repeated words and topics. This method alone cannot fully explain audience sentiment, but it is useful as a first step because it shows which themes repeatedly appear in the comments. The most important result was that emotional and relational terms appeared frequently together with plot-related terms.

Cluster Term count Representative terms Interpretation

Positive / negative 202 best, bad, seen, watch, just Shows general evaluation evaluation and viewing experience. film, plot, better, end, Shows that viewers Story / plot / ending 188 character discuss narrative structure and endings. Recommendation / strong 179 movie, great, good, watch, Shows recommendation praise story and shareability language. Performance / star power 127 performances, actor Shows attention to acting names, roles and star-driven appeal. Shows social Politics / society / satire 108 people, real, world, think interpretation and topical discussion. Action / sport / immersion 39 scenes, racing, job, cool Shows genre-specific excitement and spectacle. songs, animated, pop, Shows musical and Music / song / sound 32 demon fandom-based sharing potential. Multilingual review Shows that some reactions cluster 25 que, la, filme, pelicula came from non-English sources.

Figure 2. Full word-cluster summary from the analysis. The table shows that evaluation, story/plot,

recommendation, and social interpretation clusters were the most visible groups in the collected reactions.

3.3 Word cloud visualization

The word cloud was used only as a visualization of frequency-based results, not as a separate analytical method. Its purpose is to help readers quickly see the dominant emotional and thematic words. Larger words should be interpreted together with the frequency table, not alone.

Figure 3. Topic-centered visualization. The figure supports the keyword results by showing that emotional

themes, plot discussion, and genre-specific topics formed distinguishable groups.

3.4 Cluster scatterplot interpretation

The scatterplot maps TF-IDF word vectors into two dimensions using SVD. Points that appear close together have similar usage patterns in the comments. The dense cluster near the lower-left area indicates that many common reaction words appeared across several works, while isolated points suggest words that were strongly linked to specific genres, actors, or titles. Therefore, the figure should not be read as exact sentiment scores; it is better understood as a map of vocabulary similarity.

Figure 4. TF-IDF term-vector scatterplot reduced by SVD. The dense area represents common reaction

vocabulary, while distant points show more title-specific or genre-specific terms.

3.5 Sentiment and theme coding

Because simple keyword frequency cannot fully explain sentiment, the team combined frequency results with manual theme interpretation. Positive reactions were identified not only by words such as “good” or “best,” but also by emotionally meaningful phrases such as “sad but beautiful,” “I related to this,” or “this comforted me.” Negative or critical reactions were considered separately when viewers complained about weak plot, unrealistic characters, or boring pacing. The main evidence supporting emotional engagement is that emotional terms appeared across different genres and sources, not only in animation. For example, “lonely,” “friendship,” “healing,” “anxiety,” “family,” and “identity” appeared in comments about youth dramas, animation, and some thriller/drama works. This supports the conclusion that emotional relatability is a cross-genre factor.

Analytical theme Typical evidence in comments Use in proposal

Emotion / healing healing, comforting, sad, warm, Strongest basis for the integrated cried proposal. Relatability too real, my life, relatable, anxiety, Explains why youth-focused stories school stress can create engagement. Discussion potential plot twist, theory, ending, hidden Explains why mystery/thriller series clue, prediction remain active online. Social interpretation real world, people, society, class, Explains why socially relevant pressure stories generate debate. Explains why certain moments Shareability recommend, clip, scene, watch, best spread through short-form platforms.

4. Findings

The findings are reorganized into core findings and supporting findings. This structure makes it clearer which points directly support the final content proposal.

4.1 Core finding 1: Emotional relatability drives strong reactions

The most important finding is that emotionally relatable stories produced strong viewer reactions across different genres. Viewers often responded to loneliness, anxiety, family relationships, personal growth, identity struggles, and emotional healing. This finding is supported by both the frequency-based clusters and the repeated emotional phrases observed in comments.

4.2 Core finding 2: Shareable emotion matters more than genre alone

Genre differences existed, but the analysis suggests that genre alone did not explain popularity. Animation, youth drama, mystery, and thriller content all became more engaging when they gave viewers something to share: a comforting scene, a relatable line, a shocking plot twist, or a theory- worthy ending. Therefore, the final proposal should not simply copy one genre; it should combine emotional connection with online discussion potential.

4.3 Core finding 3: Discussion potential extends content life

Mystery and thriller series often maintained long-term attention because viewers discussed hidden clues, character motives, and possible future events. This suggests that a successful streaming project should include narrative elements that encourage audience participation after watching, such as ambiguous clues, unanswered emotional questions, and episode-ending hooks.

4.4 Supporting finding: Social context affects emotional demand

The original report mentioned the COVID-19 pandemic, but the connection was not fully explained. In this revision, it is treated as a supporting interpretation rather than a separate core finding. Stressful social conditions may increase demand for comforting or healing stories, but the dataset alone cannot prove a direct causal relationship. The safer conclusion is that many viewers expressed interest in comfort, recovery, and emotional understanding in recent online comments.

4.5 Supporting finding: Genre patterns help refine the proposal

The genre chart is useful because it shows the dataset’s balance and limits. Since animation was not the largest genre group, the final proposal should not depend only on animation evidence. Instead, the proposal should use emotional healing as the central theme and borrow strengths from several genres: youth drama for relatability, mystery for discussion, and animation-style symbolism or visual motifs for emotional clarity.

5. Integrated Content Proposal for StreamKUS

Instead of presenting four separate ideas, this revised report proposes one integrated content concept. This responds directly to the feedback that the proposal should be connected more strongly to the main findings.

Title: The Room Where Feelings Go

Element Plan

Format 8-episode streaming series Genre Youth drama + psychological mystery + emotional healing Teenagers and young adults who enjoy relatable Target audience emotional stories, school/life pressure narratives, and online theory discussion Core themes Loneliness, academic pressure, friendship, family expectation, identity, emotional recovery Each episode ends with a symbolic clue connected to Viral hook one character’s hidden emotion, encouraging online theories and discussion

The story follows a group of high school and university students who discover a mysterious room that appears only when someone is emotionally overwhelmed. Inside the room, each person sees a symbolic version of their hidden anxiety, guilt, loneliness, or family pressure. At first, the room seems like a comforting escape, but the characters gradually realize that it is connected to unresolved memories and relationships between them. The series is designed around the strongest findings from the analysis. The emotional-healing element responds to frequent keywords such as “comforting,” “healing,” “sad,” and “relatable.” The youth-drama setting responds to comments about school stress, identity, and social pressure. The mystery structure responds to the finding that hidden clues and plot twists increase online discussion. In this way, the concept combines emotional connection with shareability.

5.1 Differentiation from existing healing content

Many healing dramas already focus on warm relationships or calm daily life. This proposal is different because it adds a structured mystery engine. Viewers are not only comforted by the characters’ recovery, but also encouraged to interpret clues, predict the room’s meaning, and discuss each character’s hidden emotional wound. This combination can attract both emotional- drama viewers and mystery fans.

5.2 Data-based design choices

Design choice Evidence from analysis Emotionally relatable characters Based on repeated reactions to anxiety, loneliness, family conflict, and identity struggle. Episode-ending clues Based on strong online discussion around plot twists, hidden clues, and theory culture. Short, memorable emotional scenes Designed for TikTok/YouTube Shorts shareability and recommendation clips. Balanced tone: sad but hopeful Based on comments that praised stories as “sad but beautiful” or “painful but healing.” Uses youth drama for relatability, mystery for Cross-genre appeal engagement, and symbolic visuals for emotional clarity.

5.3 Testing plan

Before full production, StreamKUS could test the concept through a short teaser, character posters, and a pilot-scene clip. Audience response could be measured through comment volume, positive/negative sentiment, repeated emotional keywords, save/share counts, and theory-related comments. A/B testing could compare a purely healing teaser with a mystery-focused teaser to see which version creates stronger engagement.

6. Conclusion and Limitations

This project analyzed approximately 4,000 online audience reactions from 18 selected works to understand what makes streaming content emotionally engaging and shareable. The revised analysis suggests that successful content often combines emotional relatability, memorable characters, and online discussion potential. Emotionally meaningful stories attracted strong reactions, while mystery or theory-based elements helped maintain longer online attention. Based on these findings, the final proposal is an integrated youth drama and psychological mystery centered on emotional healing. This concept is more focused than the original four separate proposals because it directly combines the strongest patterns found in the analysis: relatable emotional struggle, shareable scenes, and discussion-generating clues. However, the project has limitations. First, the dataset was limited to public online reactions and may overrepresent viewers who actively comment online. Second, keyword frequency cannot fully capture sarcasm, irony, or complex emotional meaning. Third, the selected works include different genres and release contexts, so genre comparison should be interpreted carefully. Fourth, some data collection was manual, which means the sample may not perfectly represent the full audience. Future research should use a larger dataset, clearer platform-specific sampling rules, and more quantitative sentiment scores to strengthen the reliability of the findings.
